problem_summary: 2008 MCM Problem B (discrete): create a metric that rates the difficulty of a Sudoku puzzle and a generator that produces puzzles of a specified difficulty. Team #2858's solution, "hsolve," reframes the open-ended "difficulty" question as a search problem: model an expert human solver, measure how many candidate strategy-applications that solver must inspect before solving, and define difficulty as the expected number of such checks. The same solver is then reused as the validity oracle inside two puzzle generators.
models_used: Solver-based difficulty metric (hsolve): difficulty m(B) = average total number of 'possible strategy applications' the solver checks while solving — i.e. expected search/verification effort, not an arbitrary weighted sum of strategy weights

HumanSolve abstract algorithm: repeatedly pick the lowest-difficulty (lowest-tier) strategy not yet tried, complete-search its possible applications in a uniformly random order, apply the first valid one; total checks = difficulty of one run

Tiered strategy hierarchy (tiers 0-5) ordered by community-consensus complexity: Tier 0 Naked/Hidden Single; Tier 1 Naked/Hidden Double, Locked Candidates; Tier 2 Naked/Hidden Triple, X-Wing; Tier 3 Naked/Hidden Quad, Swordfish; Tier 4 Jellyfish; Tier 5 limited Backtracking (2-candidate cells, then Tier-0-only propagation)
Sudoku Solution Graph (SSG): per-cell candidate-set data structure (constraint propagation state) maintained and updated as the engine runs, the substrate every strategy operates on

Closed-form expected-search-cost model E(p,v) = (p+1)/(v+1): expected number of checks before hitting one of v valid applications among p possibilities, replacing actual Monte-Carlo random search with its analytic expectation

Standard Generator: constructive forward-filling generator — fill a random candidate into an empty cell, run hsolve to propagate all forced singles, restart on contradiction; uniqueness guaranteed by a modified backtracking rule (only act on contradictions, never 'complete' by guessing)

Pseudo-Generator: seed-board reconstruction generator — take a hard seed needing tier k+1, compute 'reserved cells' (cells unsolvable with tiers <= k), rebuild a fresh-looking puzzle from the completed seed that is guaranteed to require a tier-(k+1) strategy; disguised via random row/column/digit permutations
key_techniques: Reframing a subjective, ill-defined quantity ('difficulty') as an objective, measurable one (expected number of solver verification steps) anchored on two explicit, defensible assumptions (time monotonic in difficulty; solvers proceed by strategies least- to most-difficult)
Letting strategy difficulty EMERGE from search cost instead of hand-assigning numeric weights — the paper's central conceptual move and stated key advantage over prior raters

Lemma 1 with a clean combinatorial proof (uses the Hockey-stick identity) to derive E(p,v)=(p+1)/(v+1), converting a random search into a deterministic expectation so a single pass replaces costly sampling

Per-strategy 'Application Enumeration' counts (e.g. C(n,2) for doubles, C(n,3) for triples/Swordfish, sum_k C(r_k,2)+C(c_k,2) for X-Wing) — making the abstract 'number of checks' concretely computable for every strategy
Reusing the SAME solver as both the rater and the generator's validity/uniqueness oracle — one engine, two deliverables
Variance control: 20 hsolve runs per puzzle chosen because it yields sigma/mu ~ 1/10, an explicit, quantified stopping rule for the stochastic metric

Defining 'difficulty level k of m' operationally as equal-size percentile intervals of the empirical difficulty distribution over a 1000-puzzle sample — turning a vague spec into concrete numeric bounds (e.g. 4 levels => 317-598, 599-928, 929-2761, 2762-20957)
Modeling generator runtime as the expected value of a geometric random variable (1/p, p = fraction of generations landing in the target band) to predict per-level generation time
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it nails the single hardest thing about this problem: it gives a principled, defensible DEFINITION of 'difficulty' rather than a tunable fudge formula, and it argues explicitly why that definition is better (difficulty emerges from search cost, eliminating the arbitrary per-strategy weights that plague competing approaches in [15],[17]). It then VALIDATES that definition two independent ways — a Goodman-Kruskal gamma = 0.82 against 800 externally hand-rated puzzles (with a chi-square test, p<0.0001) AND a qualitative distribution match against real solve-time data from millions of websudoku.com users. Two orthogonal validations against external ground truth is exactly the credibility judges reward. It fully answers both halves of the problem (rate AND generate), reuses one coherent engine for both, quantifies everything (runtimes, variance, level bounds), and supports claims with a real working Java implementation plus a proved lemma. The work reads as honest and self-aware (clear strengths/weaknesses), which judges trust."
section_highlights: "Abstract front-loads concrete validated numbers (gamma=0.82; level-by-level generation times 0.25/3.1/4.7/30 min; pseudo-generator producing 20 boards averaging 7111 from an 8122 seed). Problem Setup (Sec 2) carefully NARROWS the open prompt before modeling — explicitly choosing an 'expert' hypothetical solver and operationalizing 'difficulty level' as percentile bands — which pre-empts the judge's 'but what does difficulty even mean?' objection. Notation section defines SSG and 'group' once, so every later strategy description stays terse. The metric (Sec 3) is presented as abstract HumanSolve algorithm first, then hsolve as its concrete realization, cleanly separating model from implementation. Lemma 1 is stated, proved, and immediately motivated by why it matters (lets them skip Monte-Carlo sampling). Analysis (Sec 3.3) is explicitly structured as a three-part validation checklist. Appendix A rigorously specifies all 14 strategies with formal conditions AND the per-strategy application-count formula — the load-bearing detail that makes the metric reproducible. Appendix B ships actual Java source. Honest, specific Weaknesses section (dearth of human-rated data; rarity of very hard puzzles from the standard generator; slow runtime)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Validation 'ground truth' is itself a single non-standard hand-rated dataset (menneske.no, 100 puzzles x 8 levels); the team concedes no standard benchmark existed, so gamma=0.82 measures agreement with one arbitrary rater, not an objective truth

The headline claimed advantage — that the metric is more objective than competitors — is asserted but, as the Weaknesses section admits, could NOT be conclusively demonstrated due to lack of real solve-time data; the websudoku comparison is only qualitative (shape/fat-tail), not a fitted correlation

The expert-solver model ignores human intuitive pattern recognition entirely (explicitly assumed away) and assumes a uniformly random search order over applications — both are strong simplifications of real human solving

Difficulty 'levels' depend on a 1000-puzzle sample assumed representative of all Sudoku; the empirical distribution (and hence the level bounds) is sourced from a particular puzzle site and may not generalize
Standard generator rarely produces hard puzzles (expected level-4 time blows up to 30 min and worse for finer level splits) — a genuine coverage failure
Pseudo-generator does not truly generate: it permutes/reconstructs from existing hard seeds and CANNOT create new final configurations, so output diversity is bounded by the seed database it lacks
Runtime (~7.8 s/puzzle to rate, 0.15 min/board to generate) is admittedly slower than existing tools
Backtracking is treated as just another (top) tier rather than acknowledging that puzzles requiring deep guessing break the 'logical-strategy-cost' framing
reusable_patterns: Turn a subjective/ill-defined target metric into 'expected cost for a modeled agent to succeed' — define difficulty/quality as the work a simulated solver expends, so values emerge from mechanism rather than from hand-tuned weights you'd have to justify
State 1-2 crisp, defensible assumptions up front and derive the whole metric from them; pick a single well-specified hypothetical user (here 'expert') to make the metric well-defined
Replace an expensive Monte-Carlo expectation with a proved closed-form whenever possible (E[checks]=(p+1)/(v+1)) — cleaner, faster, and more rigorous; lean on standard combinatorial identities

Validate any novel metric against external ground truth TWO independent ways (a correlation/association statistic like Goodman-Kruskal gamma + chi-square, AND a distribution-shape match to large-scale real-world data)
Pick and JUSTIFY a sampling/iteration count via an explicit precision target (20 runs because sigma/mu ~= 0.1) rather than an unexplained number
Operationalize a vague spec ('a hard puzzle', 'difficulty level k') as empirical percentile intervals over a representative sample, yielding concrete numeric bounds you can generate against
Reuse one core engine for multiple deliverables (rater = generator's uniqueness oracle) for coherence and economy
Model 'time to generate a sample meeting a constraint' as a geometric random variable (expected tries = 1/p) to predict and report runtime per difficulty level

Build a tiered, ordered library of domain strategies and give each a concrete cost/enumeration formula so the metric is fully reproducible — put the exhaustive formal specs in an appendix to keep the main body readable
Be candid in Strengths/Weaknesses about exactly which claimed advantage you could NOT prove and why (missing data) — honesty reads as credibility to judges
